1: Isaiah 25:1 opens the communal thanksgiving song that responds to the cosmic judgment of Isaiah 24. 'YHWH attah Eli, LORD, you are my God; I will exalt you; I will praise your name.' The personal covenant declaration 'you are my God' (attah Eli) is the foundational covenant formula (Exodus 3:6; Hosea 2:23; Jeremiah 31:33). 'For you have done wonderful things' (ki asita pele, for you have done wonderful/miraculous things) — pele (wonder/miracle) is the same root as pele yo'ets (Wonderful Counselor) of Isaiah 9:6. 'Plans formed long ago, faithful and true' (etsot merachok emunah omen, plans from afar, faithfulness, truth) — the long-range divine purposes, faithful (emunah, trustworthy) and true (omen, reliable/steadfast). YHWH's historical acts fulfill what he planned before history.
2: 'For you have turned a city into a heap of rubble, a fortified city into a ruin; the stronghold of foreigners is a city no longer — it will never be rebuilt' — the city of divine judgment. 'Ki samta me'ir lagal, for you have turned a city into a heap of rubble' — the undefined city may be Babylon (Isaiah 13-14), Samaria (722 BCE), Nineveh (612 BCE), or the eschatological 'city of chaos' of Isaiah 24:10. 'Armon zarim me'ir le'olam lo yibbaneh, the palace/stronghold of foreigners is a city no longer; it will never be rebuilt forever.' The permanent non-rebuilding echoes the Babylon oracle of Isaiah 13:20 ('it will never be inhabited again') and contrasts with Jerusalem's permanent establishment (Isaiah 25:6: 'on this mountain').
3: 'Therefore strong peoples will glorify you; cities of ruthless nations will stand in awe of you' — the universal response to YHWH's victory: 'al-ken yekabbedukha am-az, therefore a strong people will honor you.' The 'strong people' (am-az, the fierce/powerful people) = the very nations that had been the instruments of oppression now honoring YHWH. 'Qiryat goyim aritsim yira'ukha, the city of ruthless nations will fear you.' The ruthless nations' cities (aritsim, the terrifying/violent ones) standing in awe (yira'ukha, will fear you) = the reversal of the fear-dynamic: those who inspired terror in others now tremble before YHWH.
4: 'For you have been a refuge for the poor, a refuge for the needy in their distress, a shelter from the storm, a shade from the scorching heat. The blast of the ruthless is like a driving rainstorm against a wall' — YHWH's specific care for the vulnerable articulated. 'Ki-hayita maoz ledal, for you have been a refuge to the poor.' The four protective roles: maoz (stronghold/refuge for the poor), maoz le'evyon (refuge for the needy), machaseh mizzerem (shelter from the storm), tsel mecheorev (shade from the heat). The protective images cover both weather extremes: storm (zarem, the driving rainstorm) and heat (chorev, scorching heat). 'The blast of the ruthless' (ruach aritsim = breath/spirit/wind of the violent ones) is 'like a driving rainstorm against a wall' (kezorem qir, like a winter storm against a wall) — the wall YHWH provides deflects the storm.
5: 'You silence the clamor of the foreigners as drought is broken by cloud-shadow; you subdue the triumph of the ruthless' — the divine reduction of threatening noise: 'ke'orev chorev tsal av, like the drought-heat broken by cloud-shadow.' The cloud-shadow that suddenly moderates oppressive heat is the image for YHWH silencing/moderating the 'clamor of the foreigners' (sheon zarim, the noise/tumult of strangers). 'Zimrat aritsim ta'aneh, the song of the ruthless you will humble/silence' (ta'aneh, can mean both 'answer' and 'humble/bend down'). The victory song of the violent is reduced to humble silence before YHWH's power.
6: 'On this mountain the LORD of hosts will prepare a feast for all peoples — a feast of rich food and well-aged wines, of choice meat full of marrow, of the finest strained vintages' — the eschatological banquet on Mount Zion: 'veasah YHWH tseva'ot lekhol-haammim bahar hazeh, YHWH of hosts will make for all peoples on this mountain.' 'A feast of rich food' (mishte shemanim, a feast of fattened things — the finest cuts, full marrow) and 'well-aged wines' (mishte shemarim, a feast of aged wines — wines settled on their lees for maximum richness). The feast 'for all peoples' (lekhol-haammim) = universal invitation, not just Israel. Revelation 19:9 ('Blessed are those invited to the wedding supper of the Lamb') and Matthew 22:1-14 (wedding banquet parable) draw on this. The choice food and aged wine represent the full covenant satisfaction — the shalom of abundance shared universally.
7: 'On this mountain he will destroy the shroud that is cast over all peoples, the sheet that is spread over all nations' — the shroud/burial cloth as the death-covering over all humanity. 'Billa et-penei hallot haluot al-kol-haammim, he will swallow/destroy the surface of the shroud covering all peoples.' The hallot (shroud/veil) and massekha (woven covering) are the burial wrappings of death. Death has covered all peoples with the same burial cloth — the universal mortality that has been humanity's fate since Genesis 3:19 ('dust you are and to dust you shall return'). YHWH 'destroys' (billa, swallows — the same verb used in Isaiah 25:8 for swallowing death itself) this universal death-covering.
8: 'He will swallow up death forever. The Lord GOD will wipe away every tear from every face; the disgrace of his people he will remove from all the earth — for the LORD has spoken' — the pivotal resurrection-and-restoration verse. 'Billa hammavet lanetsach, he will swallow up death unto forever/victory.' Paul quotes this in 1 Corinthians 15:54: 'When the perishable has been clothed with the imperishable, and the mortal with immortality, then the saying that is written will come true: Death has been swallowed up in victory.' Revelation 21:4 draws directly on Isaiah 25:8b: 'He will wipe every tear from their eyes. There will be no more death or mourning or crying or pain.' The 'disgrace' (cherpah, reproach/shame) removed from all the earth — the shame of Israel's defeat and exile permanently removed. The closing 'for YHWH has spoken' (ki YHWH diber) authenticates this seemingly impossible promise.
9: 'In that day they will say: This is our God; we waited for him, and he has saved us. This is the LORD; we waited for him. Let us rejoice and be glad in his salvation' — the recognition-and-response formula of the saved community. 'Hineh elohenu zeh, behold, this is our God!' — the deictic (pointing) 'this is' of recognition at the moment of fulfillment. The double qivvinu (we waited/hoped): 'we waited for him and he will save us; we waited for him.' The waiting theology of the psalms (Psalms 25:5; Isaiah 27:14; Isaiah 33:20; Isaiah 37:7; Isaiah 40:1; Isaiah 130:5-6) fulfilled — the hope that sustained the community through judgment is now validated. 'Vegilah venismecha bishuato, and we will rejoice and be glad in his salvation' — the twin verbs of joy (gil = exult; samach = rejoice) expressing complete satisfaction.
10: 'For the hand of the LORD will rest on this mountain, but Moab will be trampled in place, as straw is trampled down into a dung heap' — the contrast: Zion receives YHWH's resting hand; Moab receives trampling. 'Ki-tanuach yad YHWH bahar hazeh, for the hand of YHWH will rest on this mountain' (nuach = to rest/settle, the shabbat-rest vocabulary). The Moab contrast: Moab 'will be trampled in its place' (yedushshun, will be threshed/trampled) 'as straw is trampled in a dung-heap' (bemei madmenah, in the place of dung). The Moab oracle embedded here connects to the Moab oracles of Isaiah 15-16 and Isaiah 16:5 (where the messianic throne is promised) — Moab's pride (Isaiah 16:6) is specifically addressed by the universal judgment while YHWH's mountain receives his blessing.
11: 'He will spread his hands over Moab as a swimmer spreads his arms to stroke through water, and he will bring down their pride despite all their struggling' — the swimming imagery for YHWH's control of Moab: 'vefaras yadaiv beqirbo ka'asher yifros hashoheh lischot, he will spread his hands in the midst of it as a swimmer spreads out to swim.' The swimmer's arm-spreading controls the water — YHWH exercises the same control over Moab. 'Vehishpil ge'onotav im-arbbot yadaiv, he will bring low their pride with the craft of his hands' (im-arbbot yadaiv, with the trickery/cleverness of his hands = despite their struggling/scheming). The pride (ge'onotav) of Moab (Isaiah 16:6: 'Moab's arrogance — his immense pride') specifically targeted.
12: 'The high fortifications of your walls he will pull down, lay low, and cast to the ground — to the very dust' — the complete demolition of Moab's defenses: 'umivsach migbeh chomotaiv, and the stronghold of the height of your walls' — the highest, most impregnable fortifications. 'Hishpil higbiah, he will bring low, make high ones low' (the classic reversal of high-and-low throughout Isaiah). 'Higgiya le'afar, he will bring it to the dust' — the same 'to the dust' as Isaiah 25:5 (to the ground, to the very dust). Kir-haraseth (the great Moabite fortress city, modern Kerak) on its hilltop is the specific referent — its high walls pulled down to the dust.
